query: common odontogenic tumors in relation to unerupted teeth in the anterior maxilla

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- History and Physical -- Ameloblastoma
retrieved_docs.content: Other tumors include adenomatoid odontogenic tumors, an uncommon tumor representing 3% to 7% of all odontogenic tumors. Most cases are diagnosed in the second decade of life  and show female sex predilection. It often affects the anterior maxilla. [47] [48]

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- History and Physical -- Ameloblastoma
retrieved_docs.content: Clinically, it appears as a small, well-circumscribed lesion (less than 3 cm) around an unerupted tooth. A calcifying epithelial odontogenic tumor (Pindborg tumor) is a rare tumor that accounts for less than 1% of the total number of odontogenic tumor cases. It mainly affects patients between the age of 30 and 50 years of age. However, fewer cases have been reported with a wider age range. [49] Mandible seems to be the most affected area, with around 66% of cases. [50]

retrieved_docs.title: Non-Odontogenic Cysts -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Nasopalatine duct cysts are the most common non-odontogenic cyst found within the anterior maxilla. [6] There is a 3 to 1 predilection for males; however, there is no significant correlation between the size of these cysts and the patient’s gender. [7] The mean age of patients presenting with nasopalatine duct cysts is 47 years of age. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Review the pathophysiology of odontogenic tumors of the jaw. Outline the clinical presentation of the most common odontogenic tumors of the jaw. Describe the imaging findings associated with each tumor. Summarize the available treatment options when dealing with different odontogenic tumors. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Non-Odontogenic Cysts -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Nasopalatine duct cysts present as well-circumscribed radiolucencies noted in the anterior maxilla. Oftentimes, the nasal spine is superimposed on the image, giving the lesion a “heart-shaped” appearance. Clinically, these lesions can present as painless, firm swellings on the anterior hard palate crossing the midline with or without drainage. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- History and Physical -- Malignant Odontogenic Tumors
retrieved_docs.content: Clear cell odontogenic carcinoma is another uncommon malignant tumor that usually affects elderly females in their 5th to 7th decades of life. It usually manifested as a painful swelling of either the mandible or maxilla, and it is frequently associated with the loosening of teeth. It is potentially aggressive with the potential of recurrence. [29] Primary intraosseous carcinoma mainly affects men (70% of cases), and the mean age is 52 years. The mandible is the most common site to be affected (92% of cases). It has an aggressive behavior with a 4-year survival of 40%. [51]

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- History and Physical -- Odontogenic Tumors That Arise From Both Odontogenic Epithelium and Odontogenic Ectomesenchyme, With or Without Dental Hard Tissue Formation
retrieved_docs.content: Ameloblastic fibroma tends to affect the younger population with an average age of 14. The posterior mandible is the most affected site in 70% of cases. It may be asymptomatic if small lesions, while larger lesions usually cause painless swelling. [23] Finally, odontoma is the most common odontogenic tumor. The affected patients are usually in their first or second decade of life. The majority of cases are asymptomatic. Compound odontoma usually affects the anterior maxilla, while complex odontoma has a greater tendency to affect the posterior maxilla and mandible. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Pathophysiology -- Odontogenic Tumors That Arise Only From Odontogenic Epithelium Without Odontogenic Ectomesenchyme
retrieved_docs.content: Other types in this category include calcifying epithelial odontogenic tumors (Pindborg tumor), which usually arises from the remnants of the dental lamina or reduced enamel epithelium, and squamous odontogenic tumors that usually originate from the remnants of the dental lamina. [16] The adenomatoid odontogenic tumor usually arises from odontogenic epithelium around the crowns of unerupted teeth. It is considered by many as a hamartoma. It was previously considered as a variant of ameloblastoma named adenoameloblastoma. However, a better understanding of the biological behavior of this tumor changed this concept. It is biologically non-aggressive. [17] [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Non-Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- History and Physical -- Fibrous Dysplasia
retrieved_docs.content: Fibrous dysplasia presents as painless, asymmetric bony expansion in children and adolescents. Its onset tends to occur with periods of high skeletal growth and will often stabilize as skeletal maturity is obtained. [33] Differing functional consequences will occur depending on the bones affected. Malocclusion is often seen with the displacement of teeth, splaying of roots, and crowding. The maxillary sinus may ossify, but more concern focuses on the commonly co-affected zygoma, sphenoid bone, frontal bone, and orbit. Significant fibrous dysplastic changes in this region can lead to blindness. [40]

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Surgery is the mainstay of treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Cone Beam Computed Tomography -- Clinical Significance -- Pathological Lesions of The Jaws
retrieved_docs.content: Cone beam computed tomography may help evaluate large odontogenic and non-odontogenic cysts and benign tumors of the jaws. CBCT can show the extent of the lesion and the closeness to essential structures, such as the maxillary sinus; it may also help plan the surgical approach. [8] It is noteworthy that because CBCT provides very little information on soft tissues, it should not be implemented if there is a suspicion of malignancy. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- History and Physical -- Ameloblastoma
retrieved_docs.content: The second most common type is the Uni-cystic ameloblastoma (6% of cases). It mainly affects younger patients with an average age of 22.1. More than 90% of cases are found in the molar part of the mandible. This is followed by the peripheral type (2% of cases). It may occur over a wide age range with an average of 52. Clinically, it presents as a small (less than 1.5cm), non-ulcerated sessile or pedunculated lesion. Most cases arise from the posterior gingival mucosa. [38] Finally, Malignant ameloblastoma behaves like the solid or multicystic type.

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: The complications can be either related to the tumor or surgical management. Gradual expansion of the tumor may lead to mandibular maxillary distortion leading to facial asymmetry, pain, teeth displacement, malocclusion, or recurrence. Distant metastases can be seen in cases of malignant tumors. Lungs and cervical lymph nodes are the most affected sites.

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Evaluation -- Ameloblastoma
retrieved_docs.content: Other tumors include adenomatoid odontogenic tumor that usually appears as a well-circumscribed unilocular translucency around an unerupted tooth, usually a canine. [47] [48] A calcifying epithelial odontogenic tumor (Pindborg tumor) can be either unilocular or multi-locular. It usually appears as a radiolucent area with flecks of radio-opacities with an indistinct line of demarcation from the unaffected bone. It may be associated with an impacted tooth. [55] (Fig. 4) Finally, squamous odontogenic tumors that are usually unilocular in small lesions and multilocular in large ones and they may displace the affected tooth. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- History and Physical -- Malignant Odontogenic Tumors
retrieved_docs.content: NOS sclerosing odontogenic carcinoma affects a wide range of ages between 30 to 70 years of age with no sex predilection. Premolar and molar areas of the mandible are the most common sites. [52] It is the diagnosis of exclusion as it can mimic other head and neck tumors.

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Tumors of the Jaws -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Odontogenic tumors of the jaw are a group of lesions that originate from remnants of epithelium or ectomesenchyme associated with teeth development. It ranges from hamartomas to true tumors. The fourth edition of WHO histological typing of odontogenic tumors classifies them as benign and malignant. Then, they are subdivided according to the cell of origin to epithelial, ectomesenchymal, or mixed. [1]

retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2709
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is identified as a specific form of malignant tumor in the sublingual gland, as indicated by its inverse_isa relationship to ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان. Both Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland and the broader category Neoplasm, uncertain whether benign or malignant share the relationship termed النقيلة, suggesting a common classification or characteristic within neoplastic disease. Recognizing that the metastatic malignant neoplasm to the sublingual gland represents a subset of malignant sublingual gland tumors underscores its aggressive potential and the need for thorough evaluation, while the ambiguous neoplasm category highlights the importance of histopathological assessment to determine benign versus malignant status. The linking term النقيلة therefore serves as a unifying descriptor that aids clinicians in diagnosing, categorizing, and managing these related neoplastic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a direct procedure performed on zuby (teeth), indicating that the teeth are the specific anatomical site for this dental intervention. Disorders of teeth and jaw are described as having zuby as their parent category, meaning that conditions affecting the teeth and jaw are organized under the broader classification of zuby. Consequently, zuby functions both as the procedural target for single‑tooth extraction and as the foundational element within the hierarchy of dental and jaw disorders, underscoring its central importance in dental treatment and pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1126
retrieved_docs.content: Cancer of bone and connective tissue is linked to the broader Arabic concept “ورم العظام” (bone tumor) and is specifically mapped to “ورم خبيث في الفك العلوي” (malignant tumor of the upper jaw). In addition, the Arabic term “سرطان العظم” (bone cancer) is classified as a malignant tumor of the upper jaw. Together, these relationships indicate that cancer of bone and connective tissue is understood as a type of malignant upper‑jaw tumor, connecting the general notion of a bone tumor with a precise anatomical manifestation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179
retrieved_docs.content: Melanomas of skin are malignant neoplasms that arise from melanocytes in the cutaneous tissue and represent a specific form of skin cancer. سرطان الشفة الخبيث, which translates to malignant lip cancer, is a distinct malignant tumor that originates in the lip and is classified as an oral cavity cancer. Although both conditions are cancers, they affect different anatomical sites and are not subtypes of one another; each requires separate diagnostic and therapeutic approaches tailored to its location and biological behavior. Understanding the differences between melanomas of skin and سرطان الشفة الخبيث is important for accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment planning, and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 1217
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to frontal bone is classified as a malignant neoplasm of frontal bone. The broader category cancer of bone and connective tissue is mapped to malignant neoplasm of frontal bone and also corresponds to the Arabic term ورم خبيث في الفك العلوي (malignant tumor of the upper jaw). Additionally, the Arabic term سرطان العظم (bone cancer) classifies ورم خبيث في الفك العلوي, indicating that bone cancer includes malignant tumors of the upper jaw. These interrelated concepts show that metastatic malignant neoplasm to the frontal bone is a specific manifestation within the spectrum of bone and connective tissue cancers, and that both English and Arabic terminology align these entities.
